Facts.text: Battle of Raphia: Ptolemy IV of Egypt defeats Antiochus III the Great of the Seleucid kingdom
Battle of Pydna: Romans under Lucius Aemilius Paullus defeat and capture Macedonian King Perseus, ending the Third Macedonian War
Council of Ephesus (3rd ecumenical council) opens to settle dispute between Nestorius, Bishop of Constantinople and Cyril, Bishop of Alexandria
Pope Stephen IV, [V] elected to succeed Leo III
Pope Clement IV commissions English philosopher Roger Bacon to begin "writings and remedies for current conditions" ie. write a summary of the sciences (results in his 1267 "opus major")
Fictional character of "The Hobbit" Bilbo Baggins returns to his home at Bag End, Shire Reckoning
Antitax insurrection in Cornwall suppressed at Blackheath
Battle of Sirhind: Mughal army defeats the Suri Empire in the Punjab, reestablishing the Mughal Empire and Sultan Humayun
Jewish quarter of Prague burned and looted
Battle of Sisak: Christian Habsburg troops defeat the Bosnian Ottoman army under Hasan Pasha, in Central Croatia, wiping out the army and killing their leader
Battle at Bothwell Bridge on Clyde: Duke of Monmouth beats Scots
King Frederik II of Prussia ends torture and guarantees religion & freedom of the press
Bonnie Prince Charles sails to Scotland
Somerset v Stewart court case finds slavery unsupported by English common law, encouraging the abolitionist movement
British parliament accepts Quebec Act, which extends the province's territory and restores French civil law
1st Continental currency issued ($3,000,000)
Britain and Russia decide to invade Batavian Republic
British board USS Chesapeake, a provocation leading to War of 1812
First match at the current Lord's Cricket Ground, MCC vs Herefordshire
British Parliament abolishes feudalism and the seigneurial system in British North America
John Howe patents pin manufacturing machine
Influential North American fraternity Delta Kappa Epsilon is founded at Yale University
First ring doughnut is supposedly created by Hanson Gregory
Beginning of the June Days uprising in Paris by French workers
Stephen C. Massett opens concert at San Francisco courthouse using only piano in California
Fire destroys part of San Francisco, including City Hall and Jenny Lind Theatre, city's seventh fire in 2 years
Battle of Ream's Station, Virginia (Wilson's Raid)
Skirmish at Culp's (Kulp's) House, Georgia
The CSS Shenandoah fires the last shot of the American Civil War in the Bering Strait to indicate surrender
Arkansas re-joins the US
US Congress creates Department of Justice
American physician Dr Andrew T. Still founds the study of Osteopathy
Game of lawn tennis is introduced by Welsh Major Walter Clopton Wingfield, who introduces a rubber ball to bounce on grass
Garonne Flood: great damage in Verdun & Toulouse, kills about 1,000
Swedish ship SS Vega with explorer Nils Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld sets out to find the Northeast Passage (succeeds 1879)
Louisville Colonels set ML baseball record with 26th consecutive loss
British fleet under Vice Admiral George Tryon leaves Beirut
Bombay plague commissioner Walter Charles Rand shot by Chapekar brothers as protest against his extreme measures to combat city's plague epidemic (dies July 3)
In China, practically the whole foreign community in Peking, including many Chinese Christians, retreat to British compounds
Chinese laborers arrive in South Africa following a severe labor shortage
Haakon VII crowned King of Norway

World's largest pipe organ debuts at its new home in the Grand Court of Wanamakers department store in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania; originally built for 1904 St. Louis World's Fair, it filled thirteen railroad freight cars
BMT, then Brooklyn Rapid Transit, begins subway service
A circus train is rammed by a troop train in Ivanhoe, Illinois, killing 68 people
Herrin massacre, 19 strikebreakers and 2 union miners are killed in Herrin, Illinois.
Spain and France fight Morocco
Cardinals pick up 39-year-old Grover Alexander on waivers from Cubs
Mel Ott(Giants) homers off Leo Sweetland (Phillies) in doubleheader
RVU, Radio-Volks-University, forms
Congress approves "Lindbergh Act" making kidnapping a federal offense (amended 1934)
NL finally approves players wearing numbers
German social-democratic party (SPD) forbidden
Harry Froboess dives 110 meters from an airship into Lake Constance at the foot of the Alps and survives
Virgin Islands receives a constitution from US (Organic Act)
Joe Louisscores a stunning 1st round KO of German Max Schmeling at Yankee Stadium, NYC to retain his world heavyweight boxing title
1st Dairy Queen restaurant opens in Joliet, Illinois
About 10,000 Afrikaner women march to the union buildings in protest of South Africa's involvement in WWII
France surrenders to Nazi Germany, with the northern half of the country occupied and the south established as the Nazi client state Vichy France
SS rounds up 31 German, Polish and Dutch Jews in Roermond, Netherlands
June Uprising in Lithuania begins, resulting in the collapse of the Soviet occupation, but replaced with invading German forces shortly after
Various Communist and Socialist French Resistance movements merge to one group
Japanese submarine at the mouth of Columbia River, Oregon
Jewish Brigade attached by British Army in WW II, forms
Boston Brave Jim Tobin 2nd no-hitter of yr beats Phils, 7-0 in 5 inn
British 14th Army frees Imphal, Assam from the Japanese
Longest shut out in Phillies history, Phils beat Braves 1-0 in 15 inn
Opening day of the Soviet Union's Operation Bagration against Army Group Centre
US troops occupy Biak during Battle of Biak, New Guinea
12" rain in 42 mins (Holt, MO)
Ewell Blackwell just misses pitching back-to-back no-hitters (9th inn)
Gene Rayburn & Dee Finch morning show premieres on NBC radio
US Congress passes revised organic act for Virgin Islands
US air patrol plane shot down above Bering sea
Kansas City stops using streetcars in its transit system
Game in KC between A's & Red Sox delayed 29 minutes due to tornado
"Along Came Jones" by The Coasters peaks at #9
American bowler Eddie Lubanski bowls 2 consecutive perfect games
Shunryu Suzuki completes his historical Japan to SF voyage
Vanguard SLV-6 launched for Earth orbit (fails)
Beatles record "Ain't She Sweet", "Cry for a Shadow", "When the Saints Go Marching In", "Why", "Nobody's Child" & "My Bonnie", in Hamburg, Germany
Moise Tsjombe freed from prison in Congo
First test flight of a hovercraft
French Boeing 707 crashes at Guadeloupe, 113 killed
Freddie Trueman ends his Test cricket career, v NZ at Lord's
South African Bishop Alphaeus Hamilton Zulu, is refused a passport and thus permission to attend an international church conference by the South African government
"Here Come Da Judge" by The Buena Vistas peaks at #88
Cleveland's Cuyahoga River catches fire due to pollution
The Derry Housing Action Committee (DHAC) stage a protest by blocking the Lecky Road in the Bogside area of Derry, Northern Ireland
Irish socialist, republican and Member of Parliament, Bernadette Devlin, loses her appeal against a 6-month prison sentence imposed for taking part in riots in Derry
Supreme Court ruled juries of less than 12 are constitutional
WSWO TV channel 26 in Springfield, OH (ABC) suspends broadcasting

Pretoria court rules that the former leader of the banned Pan Africanist Congress (PAC), Robert Sobukwe, will not be allowed to use his exit permit to leave South Africa for study in the United States
The Irish Republican Army announce that it would call a ceasefire from 26 June 1972 provided that there is a "reciprocal response" from the security forces
Dutch High Council stops fluorine addition to drink water
Skylab 2's astronauts land on return voyage from US space station - 1st to safely return to earth

Ulster Volunteer Force try to derail a train by planting a bomb on the railway line near County Kildare, Ireland; a civilian who tries to stop them is stabbed-to-death (his actions delay the explosion to let the train pass safely)
SD Padre pitcher Randy Jones ties record of 68 innings without a walk
Former US Attorney General John Mitchell starts 19 months in Alabama prison for perjury regarding his involvement the Watergate Scandal
Walt Disney's animated film "The Rescuers" is released, first Disney film to get a sequel
James Christy's discovery of Pluto's moon Charon announced
Neo-Nazis call off plans to march in Jewish community of Skokie, Illinois
Pro Football Researchers Association forms in Canton, Ohio
Jim King begins riding the Miracle Strip Roller Coaster for 368 hours
2 Habash terrorists attack a travel agency in Greece killing 2
Iranian president Abolhassan Banisadr is impeached and removed from office, several of his associates are executed and he goes into hiding
Iranian president Bani Sadr deposed
Manhattan institutes bus-only lanes
Susan Lea Hammett (18), of Mississippi, crowned 25th America's Junior Miss
1st time a satellite is retrieved from orbit by Space Shuttle
NHL institutes a 5 minute sudden death overtime period
Calvin Griffith signs letter of intent to sell ownership of Twins
Carl Pohlad becomes CEO of Minnesota Twins
Jolande van de Meer swims Dutch record 800 m freestyle (8:39.30)
Joseph Luns resigns as secretary-general of NATO
Pirate Radio Euro Weekend (Holland) begins transmitting
Spain's premier Gonzalez' Socialist Party wins elections
A bomb blast in a Johannesburg video game arcade kills an unborn baby and injures ten people
The International Labour Organisation, meeting for its annual conference in Geneva, calls for international sanctions against South African minerals
Business Day reports that SA is about to test an intermediate range ballistic missile capable of carrying nuclear warheads
Florida passes a law which prohibits wearing a thong bathing suit
Longest game in Toronto, Yanks beat Blue Jays 8-7 in 15 inns
MLB Atlanta Braves replace manager Russ Nixon with GM Bobby Cox
Underwater volcano, Mount Didicas, erupts in the Philippines
Supreme Court rules "hate crime" laws violated free-speech rights
NY MetAnthony Youngties record of 23rd straight lose
FIFA World Cup: USA beats Colombia 2-1 in round match at the Rose Bowl, Pasadena, 1st WC win since 1950
Mets reliever John Franco sets lefty save mark at 253
NHL Draft: Prince Albert Raiders (WHL) defenceman Chris Phillips first pick by Ottawa Senators
Saurav Ganguly scores 131 at Lord's on Test cricket debut
Former Mpumalanga premier, Ndaweni Mahlangu causes a storm within political circles with his now infamous statement, "It is acceptable for politicians to lie", South Africa
An earthquake in western Iran measuring 6.5 on the Richter scale kills more than 261 people.
Two Metro subway trains collide in Washington, D.C., killing 9 and injuring over 80
Cult Bollywood film "Gangs of Wasseypur Part 1" directed by Anurag Kashyap is released in India
NHL Draft: Sarnia Sting (OHL) right wing Nail Yakupov first pick by Edmonton Oilers
Two Baghdad market bombings kill 14 people and injure 106

Roskilde 6, world's longest Viking longship (37 meters/123 ft) rediscovered 1996, probably part of King Canute's royal fleet (built 1025 AD) goes on display for the first time at National Museum in Copenhagen
JAMA Internal Medical Journal announces obese Americans now outnumber those just overweight
NHL owners meeting unanimously approves the Las Vegas expansion bid to start play in the 2017-18 season.
NBA Draft: Washington point guard Markelle Fultz first pick by Philadelphia 76ers
Eurozone countries agree debt relief deal for Greece, signalling end to the country's economic crisis
10-year-old Richard of Bordeaux succeeds his grandfather Edward III asRichard II, King of England
Battle of Morat:Charles the Boldinvades Switzerland
Ferdinand of Austria and Ottoman SultanSuleiman the Magnificentsign peace treaty in Constantinople
Cardinal John Fisher is beheaded on Tower Hill, London, for refusing to acknowledge KingHenry VIIIas head of the Church of England
Cornelis de Houtman's Dutch fleet reaches Bantam (Banten), Java
English explorerHenry Hudson, his son and seven others are set adrift in Hudson Bay by mutineers on his ship Discovery and never seen again
Galileo Galileirecants his "heretical" position that the Earth orbiting the Sun is at odds with the Bible and church teaching (Vatican apologizes in 1992 for how it handled the case)
Royal Greenwich Observatory is established in England by KingCharles II
Upon learning of plans by the Americans to execute a surprise attack,Laura Secordwalks 32 km to warn British troops, results in a British surprise victory at the Battle of Beaver Dams
After his defeat at the Battle of Waterloo,Napoleon Bonaparteabdicates for the second time, in favor of his sonNapoleonII
Barnburners (anti-slavery) party nominatesMartin Van Burenfor President
Acknowledged as 1st Class Cricket debut of DrW. G. Grace; as a 16-year-old he is dismissed for 0 playing for Gentlemen of South v Players of South at Kennington Oval, London
KingGeorge Vcrowned King of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, Canada, Australia, South Africa, New Zealand, and all his realms and territories beyond the sea
Paavo Nurmiruns world record 10,000m (30:40.2)
New York Yankees first basemanLou Gehrigsmashes 3 HRs in 20-13 win over Philadelphia A's at Shibe Park
John Dillingeris informally named America's first Public Enemy Number One
ChallengerJoe LouisKOsJames J. Braddockin the 8th round at Chicago's Comiskey Park for the world heavyweight boxing title
Princess and future Queen Elizabeth meets future husbandPrince Philipof Greece (Midshipman Mountbatten, RN)
European broadcast première ofDmitri Shostakovich's 7th Symphony in London conducted by Sir Henry J. Wood and the London Philharmonic Orchestra
617 Squadron (Dambusters) attends investiture at Buckingham Palace; Commanding OfficerGuy Gibsonawarded the Victoria Cross
US PresidentFranklin Rooseveltsigns "GI Bill of Rights" (Servicemen's Readjustment Act)
Speaking at a prayer meeting in New Delhi,Gandhicalls on the South African government to stop 'hooliganism' by Whites
Ezzard CharlesbeatsJersey Joe Walcottin 15 for National Boxing Association world heavyweight title, at Comiskey Park in Chicago, Illinois
Famous New Zealand murder case; schoolgirls Pauline Parker and Juliet Hume murder Pauline's mother Honora Parker in Christchurch; portrayed inPeter Jackson's film "Heavenly Creatures"
Antonio Segniforms government in Italy
Walt Disney's animated film "Lady & the Tramp" released
"Class" the first hit byChubby Checkerpeaks at #38 in the US
Most Phillies strike out in a game (16 bySandy Koufax)
MLB Chicago Cubs'Ernie Banksends 717 consecutive games played streak
Tony Sheridan& the Beatles, billed as The Beat Brothers, record "My Bonnie" & "The Saints", produced by Bert Kaempfert, in Hamburg, Germany
"Little"Stevie Wonderaged 13 releases his first single "Fingertips" (first live non-studio recording to go to No. 1 on Billboard)
PresidentRichard Nixonsigns extension of the 1965 Voting Rights Act that requires voting age at 18 in all federal, state, and local elections
Reprise Records releases "Blue", Canadian singer-songwriterJoni Mitchell's 4th studio album
George Harrisonreleases his fourth studio album "Living in the Material World" in the UK
Larry HolmesTKOsMike Weaverin 12 rounds for heavyweight boxing title
Little Richardquits rock & roll a second time for religious pursuits
PopeJohn Paul IIbeatifies Kateri Tekakwitha, making her the 1st Native American to be beatified
John McEnroe's famous "You cannot be serious" rant in first-round win over Tom Gullikson at Wimbledon
Mark David Chapman pleads guilty to killing former BeatleJohn Lennon
MLB Philadelphia Phillies infielderPete Rosegets his 3,772nd career hit, moves past Hank Aaron into 2nd place
Prince Charles andDiana, Princess of Walestake their first new-born sonPrince Williamhome from hospital
Richard Branson's Virgin Atlantic Airways commences operations with flight from Gatwick to Newark
"Smuggler's Blues" byGlenn Freypeaks at #12
Tom Seaverretires after 3rd try with NY Mets
Adam Sandlerjoins "Saturday Night Live"
Billy Joelperforms a concert at Yankee Stadium, New York City
Nelson Mandelaaddresses the United Nations Special Committee against Apartheid in New York, saying that nothing has occurred in South Africa to reverse the ANC's position
NHL Draft: Oshawa Generals centerEric Lindrosfirst pick by Quebec Nordiques
Two skeletons excavated in Yekaterinburg, Russia identified as TsarNicholas IIand Tsarina Alexandra
Wilson Pickettpleads guilty to auto assault due to drunk driving
Ken Griffey Jr.breaks Ruth's record for most HRs by end of June (31)
Michael Moorerbeats Axel Shultz in 11 for IBF heavyweight boxing title
Film "The Devil Wears Prada" based starringMeryl Streep,Anne HathawayandEmily Bluntpremieres in LA
Patrick Kaneis selected by the Chicago Blackhawks as the first overall pick in the 2007 NHL Entry Draft
Danielle Steel’s novel “Family Ties” is published
After hiding for 16 years, Boston gangsterWhitey Bulgeris arrested outside an apartment in Santa Monica, California
South Carolina GovernorNikki Haleycalls for the removal of the Confederate flag from statehouse grounds in wake of killings in a Charleston church
Prince Harryclaims no one in UK royal family wants to be King or Queen in article published in Newsweek
ComedianJohn Oliveris blocked on Chinese social media after parody of Chinese leaderXi Jinping
Operation Barbarossa: Nazi Germany and its allies invade the Soviet Union during World War II, the largest military operation in history
Walter Hungerford, English soldier, diplomat and influential politician (fought with Henry V at Agincourt), born in England (d. 1449)[1]
Pablo Bruna, Spanish blind composer, born in Daroca, Spain (d. 1679)
Lambert Pietkin, Flemish baroque composer, born in Liège, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1696)
Jean Philippe Eugène de Mérode, Belgian nobleman (Marquis of Westerloo), born in Brussels, Dutch Republic (d. 1732)
Ebenezer Erskine, Scottish religious dissenter who established the Secession Church, born in Scotland (d. 1754)
Francesco Manfredini, Italian Baroque composer, violinist, and maestro di cappella (St. Phillip's Cathedral, 1727-65), born in Pistoia, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1762)
John Taylor, English classical scholar, born in Shrewsbury, England (d. 1766)
Lord John Sackville, English politician (MP Tamworth 1734-47) and cricketer (Kent CCC), born in England (d. 1765)
Jacques Delille, French poet and translator (The Gardens': A Poen), born in Aigueperse, France (d. 1813)
Luigi Tomasini, Italian violinist and composer (head of Prince Esterházy's court orchestra), born in Pesaro, Papal States (d. 1808)
Heinrich Gottfried Reichard, German composer, born in Schleiz, Imperial County of Reuss, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1801)
George Vancouver, British explorer (surveyed Pacific coast from San Francisco to Vancouver Island), born in King's Lynn, England (d. 1798)
Étienne Méhul, French pianist and first "Romantic" composer (Le jeune sage et le vieux fou), born in Givet, France (d. 1817)
Wilhelm von Humboldt, German philosopher and statesman, born in Potsdam, Prussia (d. 1835)
Tokugawa Ieyoshi, 12th Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1837-53), born in Edo Castle, Japan (d. 1853)
Giuseppe Mazzini, Italian politician and revolutionary (Il Apostolato Popolare), born in Genoa,  Ligurian Republic (d. 1872)
James Henry Lane, US General during Civil War (Union) and Senator (Kansas), born in Lawrenceburg, Indiana (d. 1866)
Frédéric Louis Ritter, French composer and conductor, born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1891)
Theodor Leschetizky, Polish pianist, composer, and teacher (Contes de Jeunesse), born in Łańcut, Austrian Poland (d. 1915)
Ernst Debes, German cartographer (Baedeker), and namesake of a lunar crater, born in Neukirchen, Saxony, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1923)
Richard Seddon, New Zealand politician (longest serving Prime Minister of New Zealand, 1893-1906), born in Eccleston, England (d. 1906)
Tom Dula, American folk character controversially executed for murder (subject of folk song Tom Dooley), born in Wilkes County, North Carolina (d. 1868)
Sam Morris, Australian cricket all-rounder (1 Test; 1st black & of West Indian origin to rep. Australia in a Test), born in Hobart, Australia (d. 1931)
Henry Rider Haggard, British author (King Solomon's Mines, She, Dawn), born in Bradenham, England (d. 1925)
Frank Damrosch, German-American author and music teacher (founded NY Musical Institute of Musical Art, later Julliard), born in Breslau, Silesia (d. 1937)
Maximilian von Spee, German admiral (Commander of the East Asia Squadron during WWI), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1914)
Hermann Minkowski, German mathematician (work on relativity - Minkowski spacetime), born in Aleksota, Kingdom of Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1909)
Hendrikus Colijn, Dutch army officer and Anti-Revolution Party (ARP) politician (Prime Minister, 1925-26 & 1933-39), born in Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands (d. 1944)
William McDougall, English psychologist and polymath, born in Tonge, Middleton (d. 1938)
H. Guy Bedwell, American thoroughbred trainer (North American champion in wins x 7; earnings 1918-19; first Triple Crown winner, Sir Barton), born in Roseburg, Oregon (d. 1951)
[Gwendolen] Gwen John, Welsh painter, born in Haverfordwest, Wales (d. 1939)
Thibaudeau Rinfret, Canadian jurist and Chief Justice of Canada, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1962)
José Rolón, Mexican composer, born in Ciudad Guzmán, Jalisco, Mexico (d. 1945)
Belgrave Edward Sutton Ninnis, British Army lieutenant, Antarctic explorer, and dog handler (Douglas Mawson's Australasian Antarctic expedition), born in Streatham, Surrey, England (d. 1912)
Julian S. Huxley, English biologist/philosopher, Darwin's Bulldog, born in London, England (d. 1975)
Harold Burton, American politician (Rep (R) Ohio 1941-45), U.S. Supreme Court justice, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1964)
Franz de Backer, Belgian writer (White Enemy; Ruben's Daughter), born in Aalst, Belgium (d. 1961)
Harry Seymour, American actor (Boy Meets Girl, Tenderfoot), born in New York City (d. 1967)
N. Howell Furman, American Chemist whose work on separating uranium contributed to the development of the atomic bomb, born in Lawrence Township, New Jersey (d. 1965)
Robert Ritter von Greim, German field marshal and WWI flying ace, born in Bayreuth, German Empire, (d. 1945)
Matthias Kleinheisterkamp, high-ranking German SS commander during the Nazi era, born in Elberfeld, German Empire (d. 1945)
Osvald Chlubna, Czech composer (Symphony of Life and Love; In Spring), and musicologist, born in Brno, Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1971)
Francis Denebrink, US Admiral (WW I, WW II - commanded the Brooklyn, Korea), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1987)[1]
Norbert Elias, German-British sociologist and philosopher (Process of Civilization), born in Breslau, German Empire (d. 1990)
Dorothy Devore, American actress (Narrow Street, Senior Daredevil), born in Fort Worth, Texas (d. 1976)
Michal Kalecki, Polish economist, born in Łódź, Russian Poland (d. 1970)
Richard Gurley Drew, American inventor (created masking tape and cellophane at 3M), born in Saint Paul, Minnesota (d. 1980)
Jennie Tourel [Davidovich], Russian-American mezzo-soprano (Paris Opera, 1931-38; Metropolitan Opera,1937-47), born in Vitebsk, Russian Empire (d. 1973)
Russell Vis, American Greco-Roman wrestler (Olympic gold freestyle lightweight 1924), born in Grand Rapids, Michigan (d. 1990)
Jack Whiting, American actor and singer (Marge & Gower Champion Show), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1961)
Naunton Wayne, Welsh actor (Quartet, Hidden Room, Double Confession), born in Llanwonno, Glamorganshire, Wales (d. 1970)
Willem Jacobus du Plooy Erlank (Eitemal), South African poet and professor in Afrikaans and Dutch literature at the University of Stellenbosch, born in Wolmaransstad, Transvaal (d. 1984)
Arnold J d'Ailly, Dutch mayor of Amsterdam (1946-56), born in Franeker, Netherland (d. 1967)
David Burns, American actor (The Music Man; Hello Dolly!), born in New York City (d. 1971)
Marguerite De La Motte, American actress, born in Duluth, Minnesota (d. 1950)
Walter Leigh, British composer (The Frogs of Aristophanes), born in Wimbledon, London, England (d. 1942)
Katherine Dunham, American dancer (Casbah; Stormy Weather), choreographer (Metroplitan Opera, 1963; Joplin's Treemonisha, 1972), and social activist, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2006)
Michael "Mike" Todd [Avrom Goldbogen], American producer (Around the World in 80 Days) and 3rd husband of Elizabeth Taylor (1957-58), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1958)
Anne Ziegler [Irené Eastwood], British singer of light opera, known with husband Webster Booth as the "Sweethearts in Song", born in Liverpool, England (d. 2003)
John Hunt, British Army officer and leader of the first expedition to summit Mount Everest, born in Simla, British India (d. 1998)
Konrad Zuse, German engineer, pioneering computer scientist, and inventor of Z3 (world's first working programmable computer), born in Berlin, Prussia, German Empire (d. 1995)
Peter Pears, British operatic tenor (Death in Venice), born in Farnham, England (d. 1986)
Wilhelm Demian, Romanian composer and conductor, born in Braşov, Transylvania, Kingdom of Romania (d. 1994)
Guus Jansen, Dutch pianist, Hammond organist, and popular song composer ("Net als Toen" - Eurovison, 1957), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1990)
Marie Braun, Dutch swimmer (Olympic gold 1928), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Princess Caroline Mathilde of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha, German aristocrat, born in Callenberg Castle, Bavaria (d. 1983)
Vit Nejedly, Czech composer, born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1945)
Wladyslaw Sila-Nowicki, Polish lawyer and advisor to Solidarity labor union, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1994)
Lilo Hardel, German writer, born in Berlin (d. 1999)
(David) "Dolf" van der Linden, Dutch conductor of popular and light classical music (Metropole Orchestra, 1945-80; Eurovision, 1957-71), and composer, born in Vlaardingen, Netherlands (d. 1999)
Randolph Hokanson, American concert pianist and teacher (University of Washington, 1949-84), born in Bellingham, Washington (d. 2018)
Johnny Jacobs, American television announcer (The Dating Game), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1982)
Davey O'Brien, American College Football Hall of Fame quarterback (Heisman Trophy 1938, Texas Christian University; NFL: Philadelphia Eagles), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 1977)
Richard Eastham, American actor (Battle for the Planet of the Apes, Falcon Crest), born in Opelousas, Louisiana (d. 2005)
Gower Champion, American stage dancer, choreographer, actor, and theater director (42nd Street; Showboat; Marge & Gower Champion Show), born in Geneva, Illinois (d. 1980)
Chandu Sarwate, Indian cricket all-rounder (9 Tests, HS 37, 3 wickets; Maharashtra, Hindus, Holkar, Madhya Pradesh), born in Sagar, British India (d. 2003)
Marea Hartman, English sports executive (President Amateur Athletic Association of England 1991-94; integrated women's athletics), born in London (d. 1994)
Paul Frees [Solomon Hersh Frees], American voice actor (voice of Boris Badenov in The Rocky and Bullwinkle Show), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1986)
Barbara F. Vucanovich, American politician (Rep-R-NV, 1983-1997), born in Camp Dix, New Jersey (d. 2013)
Joseph Papp, American stage producer and director (Pirates of Penzance; A Chorus Line), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1991)
Bill Blass, American fashion designer (dressed Nancy Reagan), born in Fort Wayne, Indiana (d. 2002)
Jimmy Cameron, Jamaican cricketer (WI batsman 1948-49), born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 1994)
Rafael "Felo" Ramirez, Cuban-American Spanish language sports announcer (Miami Marlins), born in Bayamo, Cuba (d. 2017)
Dorothy Bohm, German-born British pioneering street photographer, born in Königsberg, Germany (d. 2923)[1]
Ruth Zechlin, German organist and composer, born in Grosshartmannsdorf (d. 2007)
Ann Petersen, Belgian actress (Home Sweet Home, Pauline & Paulette), born in Wuustwezel, Belgium (d. 2003)
Ralph Waite, American actor, director and political candidate (Cool Hand Luke, The Waltons), born in White Plains, New York (d. 2014)
Hennadiy Udovenko, Ukrainian politician and diplomat (UN General Assembly President, 1997-98), born in Kryvyi Rih, Ukraine (d. 2013)
Ian Browne, Australian track cyclist (Olympic gold 2000m tandem 1956 [with Tony Marchant], born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 2023)
Teruyuki Okazaki, Japanese karate master (10th degree black belt Shotokan Karate; founder International Shotokan Karate Federation), born in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan (d. 2020)
Amrish Puri, Indian actor (Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom- Mola Ram, Mr. India- Mogambo), born in Lahore, Punjab (d. 2005)
Michael Horvit, American composer, and musicologist, born in Brooklyn, New York City
Prunella Scales, British actress (Fawlty Towers, A Question of Attribution), born in Sutton Abinger, Surrey
Bob Bennett, American College Baseball Hall of Fame coach (Fresno State 1970-2002; record 1,302-759-4), born in Atwood, Oklahoma (d. 2020)
Dianne Feinstein (née Goldman), American politician (1st female mayor of San Francisco, 1978-88; longest serving female US Senator-D-CA, 1992-2023), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2023)[1]
Jacques Martin, French TV host (Le Petit Rapporteur), born in Lyon, France (d. 2007)
Libor Pešek, Czech conductor (Czech Philharmonic, 1982-90; Royal Liverpool Philharmonic, 1987-98), born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 2022)
Vaman Kumar, Indian cricketer (leggie who only played 2 Tests), born in Madras, India
Dick Huddart, English rugby league second rower (16 Tests Great Britain; St. Helens, St. George), born in Flimby, Cumberland, England (d. 2021)
Ferran Olivella, Spanish soccer defender (18 caps Spain, 2 Catalonia; FC Barcelona 214 games), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 2023)
Hermeto Pascoal, Brazilian jazz musician and composer, born in Lagoa da Canoa, Alagoas, Brazil
Verne Allison, American singer (Dells - "Open Up My Heart"), born in Chicago, Illinois
Bernie McGann, Australian jazz alto saxophone player and composer, born in Granville, Australia (d. 2013)
Chris Blackwell, English businessman and record producer (Island Records), born in Westminster, London, England
John Conklin, American dramaturg and scenic designer (Glimmerglass Festival), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 2025)
Bobby Harrison, British rock drummer (Procol Harum, 1967), born in West Ham, England
Abbas Kiarostami, Iranian author and director (Under the Olive Trees, Close Up, Taste of Cherry), born in Tehran (d. 2016)
